ontology.foodon_gap_note: No dedicated FoodOn entry for Jianbing exists. The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is the generic crepe food product class. Jianbing is a filled, griddled savory crepe with Chinese-specific ingredients.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers pancakes and crepes as a category. No specific Jianbing entry exists in FoodEx2; this is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Jianbing or Chinese savory crepe. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: baocui (crispy fried crackers) or youtiao (fried dough fritters)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Jianbing is approximately 250 g, representing one whole folded crepe as typically sold by street vendors in Northern China.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Crepes, plain, cooked (SR Legacy) and component estimation for filled savory crepe with egg, sauces, and fried dough insert
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Jianbing entry exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for plain crepe (FDC ID 172764), whole egg (FDC ID 748967), mung beans (FDC ID 174256), hoisin sauce (FDC ID 172237), and fried dough (FDC ID 172823), weighted by typical recipe proportions consistent with published Northern Chinese street food composition data. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9) were cross-checked against these USDA component anchors and retained where consistent. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 2.5 g per 100 g based on egg and frying oil contribution per USDA data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed-Flour Flatbreads and Crepes — analogous estimate based on wheat-mung bean flour blend crepe data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Jianbing exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated by analogy to mixed wheat-legume flour flatbreads and crepes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database, where mung bean flour inclusion and high protein-fat content from egg and fried elements are known to moderate glycemic response relative to pure wheat flour products (GI ~70–75). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 61.25 g carbohydrate in 250 g serving) / 100 ≈ 39.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450 mg per 100 g; approximately 1125 mg per standard 250 g serving) from fermented bean paste, hoisin sauce, and chili oil
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Jianbing consumption or request reduced sauce quantities, as a single serving can contribute approximately 49% of the WHO-recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (estimated 65) and moderate carbohydrate load (24.5 g per 100 g; 61.25 g per serving), partially offset by mung bean flour, dietary fibre (2.2 g per 100 g), protein (6.5 g), and fat (9.5 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Jianbing occasionally as part of a balanced meal, but should monitor total carbohydrate intake per serving and request reduced sauce to limit added sugars and sodium.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (450 mg per 100 g) and moderate fat content (9.5 g per 100 g, including approximately 2.5 g saturated fat) from egg and fried dough insert
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit Jianbing frequency due to its high sodium and moderate saturated fat content, and should opt for versions without the fried dough insert to reduce overall fat and calorie load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jianbing (煎饼 / 煎餅) is one of China's most iconic and widely consumed street breakfast foods, originating from the Shandong and Tianjin regions of Northern China. It is a thin, savory crepe made from a batter of mung bean flour, millet flour, or wheat flour — or a blend of these — spread thinly on a hot circular cast-iron griddle using a wooden spreader. A whole egg is cracked directly onto the crepe surface, spread evenly, and topped with chopped scallions and black sesame seeds. Once the crepe is flipped, the inner surface is brushed with a combination of savory hoisin sauce, spicy chili oil, and fermented bean paste (doubanjiang or tianmianjiang), imparting a complex umami-rich, mildly spicy, and slightly sweet flavor profile. The defining textural element is the placement of 'Baocui' (crispy fried wonton crackers) or 'Youtiao' (fried dough fritters) in the center before folding, creating a satisfying contrast between the soft, pliable crepe exterior and the crunchy interior. The assembled crepe is folded into a neat rectangle or roll and served immediately as a portable, handheld meal. Nutritionally, Jianbing provides approximately 210 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 9.5 g fat, and 24.5 g carbohydrate. It is a moderate source of dietary fibre (2.2 g per 100 g) and provides B vitamins, iron, and phosphorus from the egg and legume flour components. Its primary clinical concern is high sodium content (450 mg per 100 g) from the multiple fermented and seasoned sauces, making it unsuitable for individuals on strict sodium-restricted diets. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), moderated by the mung bean flour and macronutrient composition.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of jianbing?

ingredient_synonyms.doubanjiang: fermented bean paste / spicy bean sauce
ingredient_synonyms.tianmianjiang: sweet fermented flour paste / sweet bean sauce
ingredient_synonyms.hoisin sauce: Chinese barbecue sauce / sweet soy-based sauce
ingredient_synonyms.mung bean flour: green bean flour / lü dou fen
ingredient_synonyms.jianbing: Chinese savory crepe / Northern Chinese breakfast crepe
ingredient_synonyms.jianbing guozi: jianbing with crispy insert / filled Chinese crepe

confidence_reason: No exact Jianbing entry exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for crepe, egg, mung bean, hoisin sauce, and fried dough, cross-checked against the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.9). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using mixed wheat-legume flour flatbread data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, cholesterol, and moisture data for Jianbing as a composite street food are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of April 2026. Saturated fat (2.5 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy egg and frying oil component data. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries (plain crepe, whole egg, mung beans, hoisin sauce, fried dough) weighted by typical recipe proportions; no single approved-source entry for Jianbing exists. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on mixed wheat-legume flour flatbreads in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Jianbing has been published in an approved source. 4. The input record listed two identical portion entries (250 g, is_default: true); deduplicated to a single 250 g default serving. 5. Jianbing is not a Singaporean dish; G6 multilingual requirements for Singaporean dishes (Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. Chinese-script forms (simplified and traditional) are present as required for a Chinese-origin dish.
